rationale.R1.2: The artifact makes specific mathematical claims — a convergence bound of the form ||r_k||/||r_0|| ≤ (2+ε)(r/|c|)^k derived by composing an existing theorem with a perturbation argument — whose internal logical consistency is naturally central to any substantive opinion on the paper.
rationale.R1.3: Empirical speedup figures (1.4–2.1×) and the recommended heuristic l = ⌈log₂ k⌉ are asserted without experimental detail in the artifact; alignment between those claims and any supporting evidence is a natural locus of evaluation.
rationale.R1.4: The paper makes an explicit first-mover novelty claim ('first sketched Krylov method with a field-of-values convergence guarantee'); assessing that claim against nearest prior work (Nakatsukasa–Tropp, Embree–Saad) is a natural part of any opinion.
rationale.R1.7: Three specific citations are invoked with theorem-level precision (Saad–Schultz 1986, Nakatsukasa–Tropp 2021, Embree–Saad 2019 Thm 3.4); verifying attribution and whether those results actually imply what is claimed is load-bearing for R1.2 and R1.4 both.

parsed.clarification_question: Is your primary concern (a) the technical correctness of the mathematical derivation and citations, or (b) the novelty and positioning of the contribution relative to prior work?
